data.title: Baytex Energy: Post Divestiture Guidance
data.description:
The previous article on Baytex Energy (BTE) covered the sale of the United States operations. The company intends to become a wholly Canadian company that reports using Canadian dollars. We will have to see if it keeps its NYSE listing after the sale closes because it will be a materially smaller company. My own feeling is that this sale is happening too fast before the results of such a large acquisition became apparent. This idea is reinforced by the announcement of a loss that will accompany the sale. Still, the stock price has responded positively to the announcement. The company will have a lower debt ratio and now has announced plans going forward without the United States operations.

The first concern was the announcement of a $750 million line of credit that will be available to the post-divestiture Canadian company.

The Budget

Shown below is a budget that assumes a corporate breakeven of roughly C$52 per barrel. This is supposedly about 15% lower than what was the case before the divestiture. Interestingly, one of the reasons given a few years back for the acquisition was it would lower the corporate cost structure. For some reason that did not happen.

This budget assumes a roughly 3% growth rate. We will have to see how that works out. The company is going back to being a mostly heavy oil producer. That may be an ok idea with the startup of the Trans Mountain Expansion Pipeline. This has contracted discounts to light oil production and made the heavy oil business more profitable.

However, just about every company I follow in this business has responded to the increased profitability by guiding to increased production (including this company). That likely means that conditions will return to what they were before the new pipeline began operations.

What looks like a good idea right now may turn out to not have been a good idea within a few years. The light oil acquisition was a big one and needed time to show results. Getting costs down and optimizing operations takes some time. Mr. Market is not exactly known for patience. But the originally assumed results would likely have resulted in better stock pricing, and this company would have had a significant premium product business.

As it stands now, there is some premium production to develop. Hopefully that production will be enough should there be a cyclical downturn that threatens the profitability of the heavy oil business. This company has had some rough times at cycle bottoms in the past. That is what made the announcement of the sale of the Eagle Ford operations such a surprise.

However, Canada does have some very cheap light oil deposits. This company does have an interest in some of those deposits. So, let us see how this idea works out. Even though I have my doubts, this could end up as the way to go.

Note that this is a lower cost situation than many of the replies to the Dallas Federal Reserve Industry Survey of the first quarter for 2025 indicated. Most of the replies back then came in the WTI $60 range. So, there could be some competitive advantages to investing in this company.

However, this company is in primarily the heavy oil business. Therefore, assumptions about the discount of the heavy oil selling price to the WTI benchmark (CL1:COM) are particularly suspect. That is usually where the issues are with this company based upon past history. That discount has the nasty habit of widening during times of weak demand. That causes shut-in production and a loss of cash flow.

Note that management wisely wants to grow the light oil business in the Pembina Duvernay. Hopefully this management expands upon this idea considerably before conditions return to what they once were before the new pipeline began operations.

However, for the next couple of years, this company could be "in the clear" as it should take some time for all the expansion plans out there to pressure the discount to the light oil WTI benchmark.

What would help the situation considerably is a pipeline or two from Canada to the refining capacity of the United States (or more refineries up north). But with this idea, I covered some practices by certain pipeline operators that turned even conservative areas against this idea. The result is that no one wants a brand-new pipeline in their backyard or on their farmland no matter their political beliefs. That has immensely complicated efforts for a realistic solution to get this production to market. There are some similar issues with refineries.

Summary

Baytex Energy is proposing to become (again) an all-Canadian company after the sale of the United States operations. What may help here is the advancement of technology that has brought large areas of Canada back into production as rich gas production rather than dry gas production.

The Pembina Duvernay is an example of an area benefitting from the technology advancement. Even though the risk of this strategy is probably a bit higher than what was going on, this Strong Buy idea still has a logical (plausible) way forward that could produce some good results.

As such, the company is worth a Strong Buy rating. The Clearwater heavy oil play has an unusually low cost that may allow for cash flow during times of weak commodity prices even if the discount widens. Now this idea has yet to be tried out with a "full blast" downturn. But preliminary numbers and research look good. Now, of course, let us see what actually happens if a recession occurs.

In the meantime, the growth of the light oil business appears to be a solid idea for decent cash flow during a cyclical downturn.

The sale will be decreasing the debt ratio (which should please Mr. Market). This could be a materially better company a few years out.

Risks

The Eagle Ford was a low-cost area to operate. Since the acquisition was large for the size of the company, it was always going to take a few years to get free cash flow (and debt) to an appropriate level. This sale accelerates that process. But it accelerates the process at the cost of a significant loss. Nonetheless, the stock price is responding positively to the sale. As to whether a certain way forward is better is open to debate.

The whole strategy may benefit from the company in that the company began as a Canadian company and is returning to being solely a Canadian company again with solid light oil prospects. Now those prospects could fail to meet expectations. But the company has some solid reasons for believing in the Pembina Duvernay after years of testing before moving to a production mode.

There is a new COO as part of the announcement. Any senior management change needs some time for investors to tell whether the idea will work or not.

A severe and sustained commodity price downturn could materially change the company outlook if this happens before Baytex Energy is ready for it.

data.title: DGRW: High Quality, Low Dividends, And Balanced Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last reviewed the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth ETF (DGRW) on March 19, 2024, when I argued that it was a solid complement to the low-growth, high-dividend Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). That part remains true, but if you were expecting DGRW to excel on dividend growth, you were surely disappointed. In my view, a few commas are missing from its name, because a read of DGRW's methodology document indicates it aims to be a quality fund, a dividend fund, and a growth fund, all at the same time. Unfortunately, that's not always possible, and DGRW's 1.34% trailing dividend yield and -0.91% three-year dividend CAGR is proof that dividend investors are getting the short end of the stick.

That doesn't mean DGRW is a bad ETF, but since capital appreciation is clearly the primary objective, it does mean we should consider other funds in the same category regardless of their dividend characteristics. In this article, I'll compare DGRW with three other low-yielding peers and conclude that while DGRW is solid, its advantages at this point are limited. I hope you enjoy the read.

WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth ETF Overview

DGRW Strategy Discussion and Current Composition

DGRW tracks the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth Index, whose methodology document notes is "comprised of dividend-paying stocks with growth characteristics." The dividend-paying requirement itself is fairly loose, as even the lowest-yielding stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) qualify, and the Index's dividend-dollar weighting scheme permits these types of stocks to control a large part of the Index. DGRW's overlap with the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has reached 53%, and, as shown below, its top ten list includes other low-yielding stocks like Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Meta Platforms (META).

Despite their low yields, these companies obviously have excellent growth and quality features, and that's really what DGRW aims to achieve. As described in the methodology document:

Eligible companies are ranked on a composite score of two fundamental factors: growth and quality, which are equally weighted. The growth factor is determined by a company's ranking based on a 50% weight in its median analyst earnings growth forecast, a 25% weight in its trailing 5-year earnings growth and a 25% weight in its trailing 5-year sales growth. The quality factor is determined by a company's ranking based on 50% of its historical three-year average return on equity and 50% its historical three-year average return on assets.

Starting with the December 10, 2025, Index reconstitution, 200 companies are selected for inclusion rather than 300 previously. In addition, the Index applies capping rules to limit the weight of any individual security to 8%, constrain the weights of individual securities to 0.33x to 3.00x their weight in a cap-weighted version of the Index, and limit sector exposures to the lesser of 20% or 2x their weight in the cap-weighted version of the initial selection universe. The result is a portfolio that has 26.54% allocated to the Technology sector, which is high for a large-cap value fund. Similarly, the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV), and the SEI Enhanced Large Cap Value Factor ETF (SEIV) overweight tech, so I thought they'd be nice comparisons for DGRW investors open to alternatives.

WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth ETF Performance Summary

DGRW Total Returns vs. VIG and SPY

A $10,000 investment in DGRW on June 1, 2013, would grow to $46,957 on November 30, 2025, which works out to an impressive 369.57% total return, or 13.17% annualized. By comparison, VIG's investment would increase to $42,154 (321.54% total return, 12.29% annualized), while SPY's would grow to $52,029 (420.29% total return, 14.10% annualized).

Despite delivering lower returns than SPY, DGRW's downside risk-adjusted returns over this period were just as good. To measure this, I calculated a 1.37 Sortino Ratio for both ETFs by setting the "Minimum Acceptable Return" to 0%. In essence, only negative volatility gets penalized, and in my view, it's a more powerful statistic than the Sharpe Ratio, which penalizes volatility to the upside as well.

The above table also highlights DGRW's 19.29% maximum drawdown, which was 4% better than SPY's, and its 7.42% "Worst 12 Months" return, which was 11% better. Certainly, these are excellent long-term results that suggest DGRW is appropriate for those looking to take some risk off the table.

DGRW Performance vs. VIG, CGDV, SEIV, and SPY

That said, DGRW's more recent returns have been less impressive, at least relatively speaking. From June 2022 to November 2025, which was the period most common to the peer group listed earlier, its 13.51% annualized return ranked #4/5 compared to SPY's 17.13% gain, which ranked #2/5. DGRW still had good "maximum drawdown" and "worst 12 months" results, but CGDV and SEIV both fared better, delivering annualized returns of 19.89% and 15.52%, respectively. In CGDV's case, its 1.77 Sortino Ratio is in a league of its own.

Besides, large drawdowns aren't always problematic if recovery times are reasonably quick. Notice how for all three ETFs, the 10-13% in August and September 2022 completely reversed in October and November. Furthermore, with all the recent tech additions to its portfolio, DGRW is slowly becoming more like SPY - consider that overlap was only 45% three years ago, versus 53% today, so arguably, DGRW has lost some of its defensive features.

WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth ETF Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for DGRW's top 15 holdings, which comprise half of its portfolio. At this level, it's slightly more diversified than CGDV (54.82%) but less so compared to VIG, SEIV, and SPY. Please have a scan of these statistics and the summary ones for each ETF at the bottom, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

#1: DGRW's Expected Dividend Yield Is 1.36%

DGRW's Index yield, which is the weighted average dividend yield of its current holdings, is 1.64%, and after subtracting the fund's 0.28% expense ratio, shareholders should net 1.36% at current prices. That's pretty close to the fund's 1.34% trailing dividend yield, but it's worth noting that VIG's Index yield is similar at 1.69%, and its shareholders get to keep nearly all of that due to the fund's low 0.05% expense ratio. For me, this adds to the reasons why DGRW simply isn't designed to attract dividend investors. Its underlying holdings' dividend growth rates and payout ratios are all healthy, but until its strategy adapts to prevent low-yielding mega-cap stocks from dominating the portfolio, investors shouldn't expect any meaningful dividend growth.

#2: High-Quality Confirmed By 14% and 32% ROA and ROE Statistics

Recall how DGRW's Index measures quality based on a security's return on assets and return on equity, so it's unsurprising to see DGRW lead the three other large-cap value funds on these statistics. However, the difference isn't substantial, and in CGDV's case, it has higher EBIT margins (30.27% vs. 29.31%). VIG is also competitive on quality, while SEIV is the obvious outlier. Its EBIT margins and return on equity are 10% below DGRW's, while its weighted ROA is only 8.63%.

#3: DGRW's GARP Ratios Are Balanced

WisdomTree ensures DGRW has well-rounded growth factor exposure by screening for estimated earnings growth (50%), trailing earnings growth (25%), and trailing sales growth (25%). This is reasonable because it's not fully banking on lofty growth estimates materializing. For example, its three-year earnings growth rate and its one-year estimated earnings growth rate are pretty close at 11.62% and 13.18%, whereas CGDV and SEIV have much wider earnings growth variances. In general, I believe this approach is beneficial in stable and declining markets, but when risk-on sentiment is significant, forward-looking growth rates, even if unrealistic, take precedent. That likely explains why CGDV and SEIV have done so well lately.

We can also measure this by calculating price-earnings-growth, or PEG, ratios for each ETF on a forward and trailing basis. Notice how CGDV and SEIV lead when dividing their one-year estimated EPS growth rate into their forward P/E ratios, while VIG and DGRW score lower:

  • DGRW: 22.40x / 13.18% = 1.70x
  • VIG: 22.35x / 13.91% = 1.61x
  • CGDV: 23.66x / 17.98% = 1.32x
  • SEIV: 12.45x / 12.92% = 0.96x
  • SPY: 24.07x / 18.37% = 1.31x

In contrast, DGRW looks much stronger, at least relatively speaking, when using its three-year historical EPS growth rate and trailing P/E ratio:

  • DGRW: 23.98x / 11.62% = 2.06x
  • VIG: 25.68x / 10.04% = 2.56x
  • CGDV: 27.84x / 11.85% = 2.35x
  • SEIV: 15.25x / 3.74% = 4.08x
  • SPY: 27.45x / 14.76% = 1.86x

Again, I believe DGRW's better balance will serve shareholders well in flat and declining markets, but these statistics also reveal how SPY is just as good from a GARP perspective. SPY's quality features are also just as good, and given the high overlap between the two, it's difficult to justify owning both.

Investment Recommendation

DGRW has an impressive long-term track record and has matched SPY in terms of downside risk-adjusted returns (Sortino Ratio) since its inception. Specifically, its "max drawdown" and "worst 12 months" figures are better, though I worry that with several recent low-yielding additions to its portfolio, the two ETFs are too similar. DGRW's overlap with SPY is now 53% compared to 45% three years ago, its estimated dividend yield is quite low at 1.36%, and due to its selection process and weighting scheme, dividend growth is not a priority.

Instead, DGRW is mainly about "quality" and "growth," and although it excels on these two factors, it ranks slightly below average from a GARP perspective, so I don't believe it has any clear advantage at the moment. Therefore, I've assigned DGRW a solid "hold" rating, and I look forward to answering your questions in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: S&P 500 Breaks Above 6,900 As Tech Leadership Anchors Rally
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

The S&P 500 is closing the year in a position of strength, extending its advance to fresh record territory as momentum, earnings leadership, and macro optimism continue to align. The index climbed into the 6,900-6,910 zone on Wednesday after posting a fourth consecutive daily gain, underscoring a market that remains comfortable pressing risk despite elevated valuations.

Unlike earlier phases of the rally marked by sharp bursts of speculative activity, the latest advance has unfolded in an orderly fashion, driven by steady accumulation and selective leadership. The tone of the market reflects confidence rather than urgency. Participation remains broad but disciplined, suggesting investors are extending exposure with an eye on policy easing and earnings durability rather than chasing short-term momentum.

Technical structure confirms controlled expansion

From a daily chart perspective, the trend remains decisively bullish. The S&P 500 continues to trade above its 20-, 50-, 100-, and 200-day EMAs, all of which are rising and well-aligned. The 20-day EMA near 6,820 has repeatedly absorbed pullbacks throughout December, reinforcing its role as reliable short-term trend support. Meanwhile, the 50-day EMA around 6,760 continues to define the lower boundary of healthy consolidation rather than correction.

The longer-term picture remains equally constructive. The 200-day EMA near 6,390 highlights how far the index has advanced without sustaining any meaningful structural damage. Each pullback over recent months has resolved into a higher low, confirming trend persistence rather than late-cycle exhaustion. This pattern suggests that selling pressure has remained corrective, with buyers consistently stepping in at progressively higher levels.

Momentum indicators reinforce this view. Daily RSI is holding in the high 50s, reflecting positive but controlled momentum. The absence of overbought readings or bearish divergence indicates that upside strength is being sustained through rotation and participation rather than leverage-driven excess. This balance has allowed the index to grind higher without triggering the kind of volatility spikes typically associated with overheated markets.

Shorter-term price action adds further confirmation. On the 30-minute chart, the S&P 500 staged a sharp recovery from last week’s dip, reclaiming intraday Supertrend support and establishing a sequence of higher highs. Pullbacks toward the 6,860-6,880 area have been met with immediate demand, signaling confident positioning even as liquidity thins ahead of year-end.

Macro backdrop and leadership remain supportive

Macro conditions continue to provide a favorable backdrop for equities. Stronger-than-expected U.S. data, including 4.3% year-over-year GDP growth in the third quarter, has reinforced confidence in the resilience of the economy. At the same time, markets remain focused on the prospect of rate cuts next year as inflation trends lower and labor conditions gradually soften.

Recent commentary has added to this narrative. Remarks from the National Economic Council director Kevin Hassett, who suggested policy may not be easing quickly enough, have reinforced expectations that financial conditions could become more accommodative despite solid growth. This combination of resilience and anticipated easing has supported risk assets into year-end.

Leadership within the index remains concentrated in technology. Shares of Nvidia (NVDA) advanced roughly 3%, alongside gains in Broadcom (AVGO) and Amazon (AMZN), continuing to anchor the rally. The artificial intelligence investment theme remains a core driver of equity flows, providing earnings visibility that has helped justify elevated multiples.

While some high-profile names such as Tesla (TSLA) have paused after recent highs, these moves have so far reflected rotation rather than broad-based risk aversion. Defensive sectors have remained relatively subdued, reinforcing the view that investors are reallocating within equities rather than stepping away from risk.

Outlook into year-end

Overall, the S&P 500 continues to trade like a market in controlled expansion. As long as the index holds above the 6,800-6,820 support band, the path of least resistance remains higher into year-end. Momentum is balanced, leadership remains intact, and macro expectations continue to lean toward easing rather than tightening.

As discussed in earlier market coverage, similar combinations of resilient growth and shifting rate expectations have historically supported equity strength rather than capped it. With trend structure intact and volatility contained, the S&P 500 appears positioned to carry its strength forward, even as investors remain alert to thinner holiday trading conditions and potential data-driven swings early in the new year.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Agios Pharma Stock: A Buy After FDA Approves Aqvesme
data.description:
It has been nearly one year since I last covered Agios Pharma (NASDAQ:AGIO), rating the company a hold. Since the stock has returned -29%, lagging the market by 47%, justifying my cautious stance. However, yesterday on December 23rd, the stock was halted following FDA approval of Aqvesme (mitapivat) for the treatment of anemia in adults with Alpha- or Beta-Thalassemia. The approval also came with a boxed warning for serious hepatocellular injury. Thus, the drug should be avoided in patients with cirrhosis or poor liver health. As I write this, shares are trading 20% higher on the news.

Even following FDA approval, Agios faces a number of significant risks. First, the boxed warning, which may limit market potential for the new drug. Second, Agios is still negative in cash flow, and the relatively low market size approved by the FDA may be insufficient to reach inflection. Finally, investors holding the stock for the longer-term are risking how the FDA will respond to Agios' sNDA application in the coming year. However, there is potentially major upside if the FDA approves Aqvesme for SCD. There remains a substantial unmet medical need in SCD, thus approval is not beyond the realm of possibility.

My take is that, given the smaller but now certain market, Agios justifies an upgrade to buy (market perform), so long as there is upside remaining to $34/share. Investors holding for the long-term may be enticed by the large upside potential for SCD, which I believe the market is currently discounting to nearly zero. Personally, I consider Agios a watch-list 'wait & see' stock, but investors accepting the risk are in a much better position to profit than when I last covered the stock.

Agios Pharma: Mixed Results in SCD

Last month, Agios plunged -49% after mixed Phase 3 results in sickle cell disease (SCD). While Aqvesme achieved hemoglobin response (40.6% of patients saw response), it failed to reach statistical significant benefit versus placebo in the primary endpoint of reduced SCPCs (pain crises). This led the market to doubt the value of the drug for SCD, although the company intended to submit an sNDA to the FDA, seeking approval in SCD based upon the "totality of data."

There is still some chance that the FDA will be receptive even though Aqvesme missed one of its primary endpoints. While the gene therapy Casgevy marketed by Vertex (NASDAQ:VRTX) and CRISPR Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CRSP) is very effective, it is a one-time ex-vivo gene editing therapy costing approximately $2.2M. The process for receiving this therapy is much more expensive and complex than taking a pill like Aqvesme. The FDA is generally receptive to drugs that, while not perfect, address an unmet medical need. Aqvesme might qualify on the totality of data argument in this respect.

This study was named RISE UP and its second primary endpoint was the annualized rate of SCD pain crisis. This rate was indistinguishable at 2.3% from patients on placebo at 2.6%.

In a key secondary endpoint, an increase was observed in change from baseline hemoglobin concentration, with the mean Hb concentration increasing for patients on the drug.

It is difficult to say exactly what chance Agios has with the FDA, but there should be major upside and low downside given the brutal selling that the stock saw in November.

Agios Pharma: FDA Approval in Alpha- and Beta-Thalassemia

Yesterday's announcement regarding the FDA approval of Aqvesme was an early Christmas present for shareholders. To put the results in context, Phase 3 data from the ENERGIZE trial met their primary endpoints. The somewhat capped upside following the press release is consistent with the fact that the market had mostly priced in the FDA approval.

The key data from the ENERGIZE trial were as follows:

  • 258 patients were enrolled, 171 on drug and 87 on placebo. 91% on drug completed the study versus 95% on placebo.
  • The study met the primary endpoint of >50% reduction in transfused red blood cell (RBC) units. Treated patients saw a 30.4% improvement versus 12.6% on placebo.
  • In addition, the study met three key secondary endpoints. These measured RBC reduction during different timeframes.

As such, it was expected that Aqvesme was approved, but the market responded positively even with the boxed warning. Agios intends to begin selling the drug by the end of January 2026, thus sales growth will begin soon, but there is no official guidance. The company has previously estimated that ~2,500 to 3,000 patients are diagnosed in the US.

Analyst Estimates for Revenue and EPS Growth

The new approval of Aqvesme is a positive, however, based on the company's own numbers it is probably insufficient to fuel profitability by itself. If Agios achieved 33% market penetration (2,000 patients) and priced its drug at $200,000 it would represent a market opportunity of $400M in the US. Agios believes that the total patient population is 6,000 in thalassemia. These numbers are guesses, but with previous rare disease approval in PK deficiency driving $44.8M in trailing 12-month sales, this leads to a peak sales estimate of perhaps $500M.

Analysts previously saw Agios requiring ~$750M in sales to reach profitability around 2029. The company has a massive cash stockpile: cash equivalents ($93M), short-term investments ($860M) and long-term investment ($304M) for a total liquidity of around $1.3B. It has total liabilities of only $101M, thus an ample cash runway to pursue commercialization.

However, $500M may not be enough, even though the company is cutting costs. Thus, there is not as much clarity surrounding an earnings inflection as I would like to see. This would change substantially if Aqvesme is approved in SCD.

Conclusions

Given the boxed warning and more limited commercial upside for yesterday's approval, I see a low chance of a complete recovery to $45/share in the near-term unless Agios is able to convince the FDA that approval in SCD should move forward despite the mixed results. However, Agios appears cheap with a market capitalization around $1.71B, offset by $1.3B in cash, equivalents and long-term investments. Leerink partners for example, set a price target of $34/share on the basis of success in Alpha- or Beta-Thalassemia alone, believing that price appreciation can be expected from ~$500M in sales for Aqvesme in this indication and PK deficiency.

Because Leerink called the approval perfectly, I believe their price target is reasonable. As such, I upgrade Agios to buy (market perform). There is some upside remaining to $34/share and there may be some chance of SCD approval, which could drive the stock back to yearly highs around $45/share.

data.title: How Likely Is A Big Stock Market Drop?
data.description:
Introduction

Is a stock market crash just around the corner? It’s a question on a lot of investors’ minds these days. In this note we’ll explore a few different approaches to answering this question, including a detailed explanation of our favorite.

Let’s start by reframing the question into something a bit more precise, namely: "What’s the probability that the U.S. stock market drops at least 30% from its starting level at any time over the next twelve months?"1

We hosted a survey that invited readers to estimate the likelihood—on a scale from 0% to 100%—of a market decline of that magnitude, and received nearly 3,000 responses. In Part 2, we’ll dig into those results and explore what they should—and shouldn’t—change about how we think about the risk of a major market decline.

Pundits

Some well-known market observers see a big crash coming – e.g. Mark "the crash guy" Spitznagel, protégé of Black Swan author Nassim Taleb, Michael Burry of The Big Short fame, and Bridgewater founder Ray Dalio. For example, in a recent interview with the WSJ, Spitznagel said: "I do expect an 80% crash…but only after a massive, euphoric, historic blow-off rally." A statement such as this suggests that Spitznagel believes there’s a greater than 50% chance of at least a 30% market sell-off coming in the foreseeable future, though he’s a bit vague about the exact timing, and the magnitude of the "blow-off rally" that will precede it. Amalgamating the statements of these market observers – even granting them credit for their past success – doesn’t seem like the best way to answer our question.

Prediction markets

With prediction markets all the rage these days, maybe we can find our answer there. Unfortunately, at the time of writing, Polymarket and Kalshi don’t have betting markets for the question we pose, though Polymarket takes bets on a "NYSE circuit breaker event in 2025". This event traded at about 16% early in 2025, and has most recently traded at 4% with six weeks to go in 2025. A NYSE Level 1 circuit breaker event is triggered if the S&P 500 declines by 7% or more versus the previous day’s closing price.

Polymarket also has a listing for a U.S. recession by the end of 2026, and it’s recently traded at a 36% probability. The link between recession and a stock market drop isn’t a tight one, and also there’s only $30,000 wagered on this event. Who knows, maybe this note will encourage the listing of a prediction market on our definition of a stock market crash – that would be a welcome side-benefit to writing this note!

Surveys

As with the prediction markets, so with surveys; there aren’t any publicly available ones that ask our question. The closest one we’ve got is conducted by the Investor Behavior Project at Yale started by Professor Robert Shiller. Since 1989, it’s been collecting responses from individual and institutional investors to this stock market crash question:

What do you think is the probability of a catastrophic stock market crash in the U. S., like that of October 28, 1929 or October 19, 1987, in the next six months? (An answer of 0% means that it cannot happen, an answer of 100% means it is sure to happen.)

Unfortunately, their definition of a crash is not the same as ours, and we don’t have the detailed data from the survey. They publish an index of the percent of respondents who attach a probability of less than 10% of a stock market crash in the next six months. As of July 2025, 40% of institutions and 30% of individuals predicted a lower than 10% probability of a crash as defined in their survey.2

A rough conversion from the Yale survey result to the question we are asking might be to assign a 5% probability of a crash to those who answered "lower than 10%" and a 20% probability to those who estimated it above 10%. This gives us a blended probability of crash of 15% over the next six months, or a 30% probability of a "crash" in the next 12 months.

Ask the LLMs

ChatGPT, Claude and Perplexity gave estimates of 10%, 6% and 7% respectively. Their estimates were largely based on the historical frequency of such declines, slightly conditioned for current market valuation levels.

History

Following the lead of the LLMs, let’s take a look at U.S. stock market history. The chart and table below are based on daily U.S. stock market price data (excluding dividends). The frequency of stock market losses of greater than 30% over 12-month periods was 7%, spread over about ten episodes. Note that we’re measuring the maximum loss over the ensuing 12-month period from each date over the past 100 years.

In the table, we show the frequency of losses conditional on the starting level of the Shiller Cyclically-Adjusted Price-Earnings ratio (CAPE). While there was a slightly higher frequency of 30% or greater losses when CAPE was greater than 30x – 10% versus 7% – it does not strike us as being statistically significant.

The wisdom of crowds – what sayeth the options market?

The options market is where we can find an estimate that has the weight of real money behind it and is also forward-looking. However, translating options prices into the probability we’re looking for requires a few steps:

  1. Find the price of a put option with an expiration about a year from today and a strike price about 30% below today’s stock market level. You can find options prices on Yahoo Finance. At the time of writing (closes as of Friday December 5th), the S&P 500 was at 6870; the price of a put option with a strike price of 4800 – 30.1% below the current S&P 500 price – expiring on December 18, 2026 was 74.0. We’ll also need the price of a one-year put option struck close to the current level of the market (i.e. an at-the-money strike).
  2. Calculate and take the average of the implied Black-Scholes volatility of these two options. Yahoo Finance provides an estimate, or you can ask one of the LLMs, or you can use an option pricing calculator yourself. For the 4800 Dec. 2026 put option above, the implied volatility is 29.8%, and the at-the-money one-year put option had an implied volatility of 18.8%. The average is 24.3%.
  3. This next step is a bit fuzzy, but important. We think it likely that the market-implied volatilities include a risk-premium, in which case we’ll want to haircut the number we arrived at above. There are at least two reasons for this: 1) stock market implied volatility goes up when the stock market goes down. The correlation between one-year implied volatility, measured by ^VIX1Y and the S&P500 over the past 19 years using monthly data, was -0.7, implying a Beta to the stock market of 1.4, and 2) arbitrageurs selling out-of-the-money put options hedge their sales with the objective of making a profit, after costs of hedging, and therefore price the options above their "fair" value. We don’t know what the exact reduction should be arising from these two sources, and it probably changes over time, but let’s go with a 10% reduction of the implied volatility we calculated above from 24.3% to 21.9%.
  4. Now that we have an estimate of the relevant expected variability of the stock market, we need an estimate of its expected return over the next year. Our suggestion here is either to use a consensus forecast of a dozen or so investment firms (e.g. Blackrock, Vanguard, Goldman Sachs, etc.) or to use the Cyclically-Adjusted Earnings Yield (1/CAPE) plus forecast inflation. We then need to subtract the dividend yield from the total return estimate to arrive at the expected price return. We estimate this figure at 4.5% (3.5% earnings yield + 2.5% inflation – 1.5% dividend yield).
  5. Given an expected return, and a variability of returns, we can now calculate the probability that the stock market finishes the year with a loss of 30% or more. The probability we get using our inputs is a 4% chance of finishing the year with a loss greater than or equal to 30%.3
  6. We’re not quite there though, because the probability we’re after is that of a loss of 30% or more at any point over the next 12 months, not just at the end of the period. It might be that the stock market goes down 40% over the next seven months, then rebounds to finish the year at down 28%. An approximation of the full-year probability is to double the period-end probability we just calculated above. To see why, imagine that for every time the market touches down 30%, there’s a roughly 50/50 chance that it finishes the remainder of the 12 months going up or down, and finishing above or below that 30% loss. This "reflection" approximation results in an 8% probability of the stock market dropping by at least 30% some time over the next year.4 Without the adjustments we made for stock market risk premium and for stock market volatility also including a risk premium, the probability of our stock market crash would be 14%, considerably higher than our estimate of the "real world" probability.

Connecting the dots

Knowledge is the antidote to fear.– Ralph Waldo Emerson

While the 8% one-year crash probability we’ve inferred from the options market is a significant risk, it is considerably lower than the probability reported from surveys of both individuals and institutions and even more at odds with the views of celebrated market observers with a bearish outlook.

What should you do with this 8% probability? First, we hope that simply knowing this probability, and where to find it whenever you want to, transforms stock market crash risk from an "unknown unknown" into a "known unknown," making stock investing a little more comfortable. We all recognize that the reason that stocks generally offer an expected risk premium above the return of safe assets is because of the significant variability of stock market returns, and so we doubt any investors will be surprised that there is a significant probability of a drop of 30% over the coming year.

What may come as slightly more of a surprise is that, using the approach described above, we find the probability that the stock market rallies by 30% at some point over the next year is 11%, noticeably higher than our "crash" probability despite 30% out-of-the-money put options being priced at 29.8% volatility while 30% out-of-the-money call options are priced at a much lower 13% volatility.5

When it comes to asset allocation, the primary drivers of how much you should invest in equities – assuming you’re only considering stocks and low-risk bonds – are 1) their expected return in excess of bonds, 2) their risk, measured by their overall variability relative to their expected return, and 3) your personal degree of risk aversion. As we explained in our book The Missing Billionaires, for typical values of the primary inputs described above, the optimal allocation to equities is affected hardly at all by the shape of the distribution of outcomes, holding expected return and variability of returns constant.6 As such, the probability of a crash – or for that matter, the probability of a loss to any horizon – doesn’t play a direct role in the asset allocation decision, and should be viewed more as an output from the return and risk distribution that drives the asset allocation decision.

As you think further about this question, we hope that you'll be guided by the spirit of Aumann's agreement theorem!

Technical Appendix

For readers who want to take a deeper dive, here’s exactly what’s behind our rule-of-thumb described in Steps 1 – 6 above to estimate the probability of a 30% drop over the next year based on the traded stock options market:7

  1. We used the Heston (1993) options model to fit one-year at-the-money and 70% of spot options prices, as well as a few other strikes.
  2. We then reduced the starting volatility in the model by 10%, and we set the drift of the stock market to 4.5% (net of dividend).
  3. Using the model dynamics with those altered inputs, we calculated the probability of the stock market being down 30% sometime over the next year.8
  4. Finally, we looked for the simplest rule-of-thumb readers could use to get close to that probability, and that resulted in what we described in steps 1-6 in the body of the article.

Further Reading and References

  • Beckers, S. (1980). "The Constant Elasticity of Variance Model and Its Implications for Option Pricing." Journal of Finance.
  • Black, F., and M. Scholes (1973). "The Pricing of Options and Corporate Liabilities." Journal of Political Economy.
  • Cox, J., and Ross, S. (1976). "The Valuation of Options for Alternative Stochastic Processes." Journal of Financial Economics.
  • Giglio, S., Maggiori, M., Stroebel, J. and Utkus, S. (2021). "Five Facts about Beliefs and Portfolios." American Economic Review.
  • Goetzmann, W., Kim, D. and Shiller, R. (2024). "Emotions and Subjective Crash Beliefs." NBER Working Paper No. 32589.
  • Haghani, V. and White, J. (2023). The Missing Billionaires: A Guide to Better Financial Decisions. Wiley.
  • Heston, S. (1993). "A Closed-Form Solution for Options with Stochastic Volatility with Applications to Bond and Currency Options." Review of Financial Studies.
  • Martin, I. (2017). "What Is the Expected Return on the Market?" The Quarterly Journal of Economics.
  • Merton, R. (1969). "Lifetime Portfolio Selection Under Uncertainty: The Continuous-Time Case." The Review of Economics and Statistics.
  • Merton, R.C. (1973). "Theory of Rational Option Pricing." Bell Journal of Economics and Management Science.
  • Rietz, T. (1988). "The equity risk premium: A solution." Journal of Monetary Economics.

Notes

  1. In price terms, not total return.
  2. The July 2025 survey response is close to the long-term average of this survey, which finds 35% of institutional investors and 30% of individual investors estimating a lower than 10% probability of a "crash" in the next six months.
  3. We’ll use the cumulative Normal probability function, assuming that stock prices are log-normally distributed. The calculation is

    N((ln(1 – 0.3) – r_s + (σ² / 2)) / σ), where N() is the cumulative Normal probability function, 0.3 is the loss fraction we’re interested in, rₛ is the expected return of the stock market price (net of dividends), and σ is the relevant stock market volatility input.

  4. Calculating this probability more accurately, taking account of the fuller dynamics of the assumed stock price distribution gives us a probability of 8.4%, pretty close to the rule-of-thumb doubling of the probability of finishing down 30% or more.A few readers have observed that we could shorten this estimation process by getting the price of a one-year "one-touch" option struck 30% below today’s stock market level. This is an exotic option that pays $1 if the stock market touches the down 30% level. Unfortunately, even if this option was publicly quoted or available on a betting platform like Polymarket, we’d still need to adjust it for the risk premium in the stock market and the volatility market, but it would be a useful price to be aware of. The same goes for using the price of a tight put spread to estimate the probability of being just below down 30% in a year – we still need those same adjustments.

  5. The primary reasons for this are: 1) the stock market has a 4.5% expected return making the call option just 25.5% out-of-the-money, while the put option is 34.5% out-of-the-money, and 2) if we believe stocks are lognormally distributed, then in log-space the call is just ln(1.3/1.045) = 22% out-of-the-money, while the put option is ln(0.7/1.045) = 40%, or almost twice as far out-of-the-money.
  6. See Exhibit 18.2, page 283.
  7. See Martin (2017) for a deeper dive on inferring risk premium and crash probability from options prices. However, our understanding, which may be mistaken, is that Martin does not adjust options volatility for a risk premium, as we do, but rather uses the risk-neutral measure of options volatility.
  8. We got a similar result using the Constant Elasticity of Variance model of Cox and Ross (1976) and Beckers (1980).

data.title: Stanley Black & Decker: Addressing Leverage Ahead Of Promised Green Shoots
data.description:
It has been a very long time since I covered shares of Stanley Black & Decker, Inc. (SWK), as I asked early in 2019: Where is the power? That power came as the pandemic provided a boom to the business and shares, but secular declines have reappeared thereafter again. Right now, the business has been facing continued stagnation and leverage buildup, rightfully pressuring shares.

This has caused a multi-year and severe period of underperformance, and while shares are not cheap, SWK is reducing leverage by selling off CAM to find some sense of stability and modest earnings recovery from here. By now, green shots in 2026 could really drive a turnaround, but investors have been lured with such promises before.

A Quick Trip Down Memory Lane

Early in 2019, Stanley Black & Decker faced real margin pains at the time, despite organic growth reported and cost programs initiated. Yet strong brand names, diversified operations, and non-demanding valuations made me average down to levels around the $115 mark.

At that time, SWK was a $14 billion business, which was driven by its $10 billion tools & storage unit, complemented by industrial & security businesses, both generating just about $2 billion in sales. At the time, the company posted non-GAAP earnings around $8 per share, with GAAP earnings only trending at half that rate, with the difference caused by integration and optimization efforts.

The company called for modest growth, with shares trading at 13-14 times adjusted earnings and likely a multiple of about 2 times higher based on GAAP earnings. Net debt of around $4.5 billion, which includes pension liabilities as well, was pretty reasonable at 2 times, while net capital investments are pretty flat and the earnings yield looked good.

A Positive Move, But General Softness

The pandemic created a windfall for the business and shares given the massive remodeling activity that took place, shares rose to the $200 mark in 2021. Since the middle of 2022, shares have been trading down to a $50-$100 range.

Meanwhile, the business has seen stagnant results; revenues of $14 billion in 2018 have come in a $13-$17 billion range in the years ever since, now coming in the middle of that range. Obviously this is very little growth, certainly if we factor in the impact of cumulative inflation over this period of time. This inflation was having an impact on the cost structure, with margins coming down as time passed, resulting in earnings being down, all amidst a pretty stable share count.

In February of this year, SWK reported a 3% fall in 2024 sales to $15.4 billion, although organic growth was flat if we account for a small divestment and adverse currency moves. Adjusted earnings were reported at $660 million, equal to $4.36 per share, as GAAP earnings only came in at $1.89 per share.

Both lower and adjusted earnings, in combination with continued dividends paid, have resulted in quite a debt buildup, for a $5.8 billion net debt load. This compares to a $1.56 billion adjusted EBITDA metric reported for the year, for a 3.5 times leverage ratio, after 2024 margins improved a long way.

Still Trending Flat

In November, SWK reported its results for the third quarter of this year, with year-to-date revenues of $11.45 billion, down 2%, in between a tiny currency tailwind and general softness also induced by tariffs. Despite these tougher times, adjusted earnings of $3.26 per share were up thirty-nine cents on the year before, with GAAP earnings reported at half that number.

Such improvements are badly needed with net debt up to $6.3 billion amidst poor cash flow conversion year to date, as adjusted EBITDA of $1.14 billion is actually down nominally on the year before, pushing leverage ratios to about 4 times.

Amidst these generally lackluster trends, it is no surprise to see 151 million shares of SWK trade around the $75 mark, for an $11.3 billion equity valuation in recent times and a $17.6 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business essentially at about 1.2 times sales and nearly 12 times EBITDA. Based on adjusted earnings of around $4.50 per share, equity trades at 16-17 times earnings, but these are highly adjusted earnings, of course.

A Deal

After some other divestments recently, SWK announced a larger divestment towards year-end. The company has reached a deal to sell its consolidated aerospace manufacturing business, also known as CAM, to Howmet Aerospace (HWM) in a $1.8 billion cash deal.

As a provider of fasteners, fittings, and engineering components to aerospace and defense industries, the deal comes at a premium. SWK reported that the unit generates about $410 million in sales with EBITDA margins approaching the high-teens, equal to around $70 million in my book. That actually feels like a very decent sale at nearly 4.5 times sales and a mid-twenty times EBITDA multiple.

This could reduce net debt to about $4.5 billion, as I see a minimal impact on EBITDA as well, as the move should reduce leverage from about 4 times to 3 times. With interest expenses trending at $320 million, the effective cost of debt comes in around 5%. That suggests that a $1.8 billion deal could forfeit $90 million in interest expenses, exceeding the EBITDA guidance of the segment. This would imply that the deal could be accretive, on top of reducing leverage, of course!

So frankly, this deal could be helpful in addressing debt, as I like the multiple fetched for the assets, but SWK will become a bit smaller. With leverage coming down, a 4.5% dividend yield now seems more stable than was the case beforehand, as these continued and elevated dividends make for limited ability to deleverage.

However, the company plays the card that margins are seen improving in 2026, with adjusted gross margins seen around 35%. This would be up a point or three from the current performance, which would do miracles for the bottom line, but frankly, investors in SWK have been disappointed (many times) before.

All this makes the situation look compelling, but it is not necessarily compelling. By now investors get paid to wait, with a 4.5% dividend yield, as the payout ratio comes in close to 75% of earnings here, but the issue is that this is based on heavily adjusted earnings here.

So, given all this, the payout ratio is really a demanding cash flow item every year, but if SWK can really deliver on further margin gains next year, it has the potential to truly deleverage. That is needed as the leverage overhang has long been an overhang. Clearly the market is not buying the story, given the non-demanding valuations, but that offers a chance as well, even if just minimal gross margin improvements flow through here.

As a result, I am quite interested to pick up coverage on SWK here, but I am not sold on the idea yet. I understand that investors are buying into this story now; I just need a few more green shoots to build confidence in a long-term SWK growth story.

data.title: Coinbase: Sell As The Fundamentals And Charts Both Roll Over
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Coinbase (COIN) initially rallied well, but recently it has corrected sharply, erasing the gains and outperformance vs. the broader market:

Thesis

I am becoming more bearish on COIN stock:

  1. Stablecoin revenue is vulnerable rate cuts
  2. FY26 is likely to see some margin pressure from increased expenses
  3. There is some valuation discount but the earnings expectations index is flat
  4. Coinbase has failed to break resistance multiple times and now looks like it might go lower
  5. An easing regulatory environment is a source of upside risk

Stablecoin revenue is vulnerable rate cuts

Whilst I still believe increasing stablecoin adoption is accretive for Coinbase and Circle's (CRCL) market-leading USDC stablecoin monetization, I am concerned that rate cuts could be a headwind for stablecoin interest income revenues earnt from holding USDC reserves. Looking one year out, we see that there is a 92.9% chance of a Fed rate cut:

I expect the lower interest rates to have an incremental slowdown impact on the rate of stablecoin interest income revenues:

As of Q3 FY25, this revenue stream makes up almost 20% of the company's overall revenue mix. Hence these headwinds are likely to impact the overall revenue outlook as well. Given this context, it is interesting to see that over the last month, there have been net downward revisions in the Wall St's revenue consensus for COIN stock:

Note that the overall revenues trend has not really had a strong directional trend over the last 1.5 years:

FY26 is likely to see some margin pressure from increased expenses

Coinbase's operating expense margins in S&M, R&D and G&A have been increasing over the past couple of quarters and this is weighing down on operating EBIT margins:

The outlook for Q4 suggests increased spends particularly in R&D and G&A due to headcount growth and acquisition impacts:

On the expense side, our expense range is higher quarter-over-quarter for tech & dev and G&A in the range of $925 million to $975 million, up approximately $100 million at the midpoint. Approximately half of this increase is due to the recent acquisitions of Deribit and Echo. The remainder of the quarter-over-quarter increase is largely due to headcount growth, which we expect to grow at a slower rate in the fourth quarter as compared to the third quarter.

- CFO Alesia Jeanne Haas in the Q3 FY25 earnings call

This $950 million midpoint guidance for R&D and G&A corresponds to a 11.9% QoQ increase. This makes me a bit queasy when the revenue growth outlook is fuzzy. I am not particularly encouraged by management's commentary that labels 2025 as an "investment year" and the initial 2026 as a period of "moderate growth" and "digestion":

As we look forward to the first quarter of 2026, we really think of that as digestion. As we go into 2026, we're digesting the growth that we absorbed in 2025. We are going to focus on scaling new products that went from 0 to 1 to get them to 1 to 10. We are going to focus on bringing everybody kind of to their full potential at Coinbase, making sure that those folks that have been with us 3 months are operating the same speed of those that have been with us for 2 years. And so we will see sequential growth Q1 versus Q4, but at a much, much more moderate level. So really think of it as a digestion year with more moderate growth where 2025 was a big investment year.

- CFO Alesia Jeanne Haas in the 2025 Goldman Sachs US Financial Services Conference

I think this is all a euphemism for potential margin pressure and lower growth in the early part of 2026.

There is some valuation discount but the earnings expectations index is flat

COIN is trading at a 1-yr fwd PE of 40.1x, which corresponds to a 59% premium to the 25.2x median of similar sectoral comp companies:

Now although COIN is more richly valued vs. these sectoral comps, I will acknowledge that the degree of the premium is less than the typical 96.7% that COIN has commanded over the other companies:

Also, the company's 1-yr fwd PE today is at a 22.7% discount to its own longer term 1-yr fwd PE median of 51.9x:

So overall, there is a case to be made for COIN's valuation looking less demanding right now. However, this may not be enough to really shift the bullish view on its own because from a fundamental driver perspective, the earnings expectations index remains rather flat:

Coinbase has failed to break resistance multiple times and now looks like it might go lower

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of COIN vs SPX500

The relative monthly charts of COIN vs. SPX500 show a whopping 4 failures to clear a major resistance level. The current Dec'25 candle looks like it is making a bearish engulf with a long top wick rejection. I see these signs as signals of a move lower.

An easing regulatory environment is a source of upside risk

The momentum from the GENIUS Stablecoins Act is flowing over as the Digital Asset Market CLARITY Act is expected to pass in H1 FY26. This bill would formally categorizes crypto assets as digital commodities, removes a lot of legal ambiguity and reduces compliance burdens. I believe Coinbase is well-positioned to benefit from these developments and extend its market leadership as a well-capitalized, US-regulated player.

More broadly and thematically, these are encouraging signs that regulators are being more accommodative to crypto and related businesses:

We've obviously, in the United States, had a sea change. I mean Europe led with MiCA. We've had leadership in Singapore to bring crypto regulation forward in other countries. But the U.S. had really, in the past administration, been quite antagonistic against the industry. So I cannot tell you what a sea change it's been, what the pendulum has shifted in the other direction.

- CFO Alesia Jeanne Haas in the 53rd Annual Nasdaq Investor Conference

MiCA is the European Union’s regulatory framework for crypto‑assets

And this can open up more opportunities for Coinbase. Indeed, it is already looking to capitalize with initiatives such as expanding into offering US stock trading, prediction markets and tokenized equities.

Takeaway & Positioning

I suspect COIN is likely to head into FY26 with underperformance as revenue growth may be challenged as the stablecoin segment faces lower yields from rate cuts and increased margin pressure from headcount and post-acquisition expenses. COIN stock valuations are trading lower, but this alone does not seem like enough as the earnings expectations index remains flat. Technically vs. the S&P500, the stock has failed to break a major monthly resistance level 4 times, signaling potential downside and underperformance ahead.

  1. Stablecoin revenue is vulnerable rate cuts
  2. FY26 is likely to see some margin pressure from increased expenses
  3. There is some valuation discount but the earnings expectations index is flat
  4. Coinbase has failed to break resistance multiple times and now looks like it might go lower
  5. An easing regulatory environment is a source of upside risk

Rating: 'Underperform/Sell'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: Solid Power: So Much Has To Go Right
data.description:
One up-and-coming, albeit speculative, sub-sector is the solid-state battery industry. The draw here for this technology is the advancement in the efficiency of these batteries. They can be much safer, last longer, and contain much more lifetime power availability than lithium-ion cell batteries, which currently dominate the market.

The applications are many, but most notably for the ever-growing electric car market. Think about it. More and more governments are pushing consumers to go all electric. While demand on the power grid is an ever-growing concern, a longer-term solution is to require less frequent charging. As a consumer, concerns over how far one can drive an electric vehicle keep many on the sidelines from buying. While charging stations are cropping up more and more, they are far less common than gas stations, for now.

We are pretty early in the development process for this new battery technology, but it could be promising for a long-term investment. Today, we highlight a high-risk but potentially high-reward growth name in this space. These are the type of plays we like to highlight in our speculation and high-risk room on occasion at BAD BEAT Investing. The name in question is Solid Power, Inc. (SLDP).

This company works to develop solid-state battery technologies. And, as we mentioned above, a solution in the battery market is desperately needed that is better than lithium-ion cells. And so, Solid Power is working on making its offerings applicable to the electric car market. Of course, when we say electric car, this includes trucks, forklifts, recreational vehicles, etc. The potential applications are many. More specifically, Solid Power is not looking to mass-produce batteries. This is an important differentiator to consider.

Instead, the company is working to be a supplier of its materials, manufacturing, and technology. Primarily, it sells a material that is a powder that is sulfide-based that serves to replace liquid electrolytes that you would see in a lithium-ion battery. It sells these materials to battery makers for them to use as they make their own solid-state batteries. The company also licenses its designs and manufacturing processes for revenue, as well as offering specialized engineering services for battery makers and even some carmakers to make their own production lines.

There are exciting partnerships on this front. So, while working in this space, it is a bit niche. And niche means the potential to maintain a moat, though there is a long road ahead, and of course, other companies competing in the battery market, all with different focuses and different technologies. As of now, we rate it high-risk and high-reward because the company is still in the very early innings of its potential growth path.

When it comes to performance, revenues are light, and the company is losing money. This is not at all uncommon for a company in this stage of the game. In the very recent Q3, revenues were just $4.6 million (and that was actually down slightly from last year). So far in 2025, revenue has hit $18.1 million. Much of this has come from its installation agreements with a major partner, SK On Company. There are also some revenues from government contracts. However, as noted above, there are exciting new partnerships that have been put into place, most notably with a Joint Evaluation Agreement.

This agreement is with Samsung SDI and BMW. It is a big step toward the future wide-scale commercialization of its offerings. Ultimately, along with Samsung SDI and BMW, the company aims to develop and supply all solid-state battery cells for integration into a next generation of electric cars. The company is going to supply its sulfide-based solid electrolyte powders to Samsung SDI, which Samsung SDI will use to build battery cells. The deal is, of course, subject to the achievement of technical milestones and performance parameters.

Now, of course, the company will work to try and get more partnerships in the future and more contracts, but this current partnership is a massive validation test of the technology. If successful, a very bright future is in sight. That said, investors here have to take this risk and have a long-term view. As mentioned, as an early-stage company, it is losing money. This is because the revenues coming in are far outpaced by expenses. Operating expense in the most recent quarter was $29 million, way above the nearly $5 million in revenue. Operating expenses are volatile and dependent upon the work happening with its deals.

That said, in 2025 the operating loss so far is $74.3 million, with a net loss of $66.4 million, or $0.37 per share. Interestingly, though, capex is pretty light, just $0.6 million, reflecting the approach the company takes that we outlined above. The most recent quarter lost $0.14 per share.

We can expect ongoing cash burn as investments are made to build out capacity and meet the requirements of its agreements. Despite the early stages and cash burn, the company, in our opinion, is disciplined financially. This year the company has invested $61.2 million into its work, and in Q3, it used another $14.9 million in cash. Liquidity is still about $300 million as the company entered its Q4, and earlier this year, a stock offering was conducted to raise cash. Contract assets and receivables added a few million. For the year 2025, total cash investment will be around $90 million.

As we close out 2025 and look ahead to 2026, the company is collecting feedback from partners on its internal cell development to refine performance. The company is also starting up a pilot electrolyte manufacturing line. It also wants to up its sampling of electrolytes to ensure performance and quality. And of course, it will work toward achieving the goals and measures it needs to hit with its partners. 2026 will be a year that starts to more meaningfully prove the concept, in our opinion. Future revenue growth will be dependent on the company's ability to draw in more partnerships and/or expand its existing partnerships. Results of these partnerships could also lead to promising data that attracts more government partnerships.

In short, this is a high-risk, high-reward play. A lot does have to go right, as organic growth of the company is dependent on the proof of its approach and uptake by the business community as well as that of governments. Projecting revenue and earnings here is difficult because it is so contingent on future partnerships and expanded relationships. However, those outcomes are also contingent on performance metrics being hit within these existing partnerships.

There are a lot of unknowns. You are also battling short interest around 11-12%, so there is this added pressure. However, that also sets up squeeze potential on continued good news. The stock is volatile, and shares have swung from the low teens in late 2021, down to under $1 this year, and back to its current level, which is the middle of a 5-year range at $4.50. That said, a lot of progress has been made in the last 5 years. The next 5 years could be exciting for investors. As such, we rate this a speculative Buy for investors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gold Clears $4,500 As Bullish Momentum Holds Into Year End
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) is closing the year in full price discovery, surging decisively above $4,500 per ounce on Wednesday and extending one of the strongest structural rallies in modern market history. The move reflects sustained demand rather than episodic fear, with buyers remaining firmly in control as monetary, geopolitical, and technical forces continue to align.

The metal’s advance has been deliberate rather than volatile, suggesting conviction-driven accumulation rather than speculative excess. With price discovery underway and pullbacks remaining shallow, gold continues to trade as a core macro asset rather than a short-term hedge.

Technical structure reinforces trend leadership

On the daily chart, gold remains firmly embedded in a dominant uptrend. Price is trading well above its 20-, 50-, 100-, and 200-day EMAs, all of which are rising in a clean bullish sequence. The 20-day EMA near $4,300 has provided consistent dynamic support throughout December, cushioning minor pullbacks and reinforcing trend persistence.

The 50-day EMA around $4,150 defines the lower boundary of trend continuation rather than correction. Even during brief pauses in November, price respected higher moving averages, confirming that selling pressure has remained corrective rather than distributive. The widening separation between price and long-term averages highlights strong momentum but also suggests any retracement is likely to be contained unless the broader macro backdrop shifts materially.

Momentum indicators support this view. Daily RSI has pushed above 80, placing gold in overbought territory, but without the warning signs typically associated with trend exhaustion. In macro-driven bull markets, gold has historically remained overbought for extended periods as capital rotates defensively. Importantly, RSI continues to rise alongside price, with no bearish divergence evident.

Lower-time frame structure reinforces the strength. On the 30-minute chart, gold continues to print higher highs and higher lows, with Supertrend and Parabolic SAR firmly aligned to the upside. Intraday pullbacks have been shallow and short-lived, repeatedly finding support near prior breakout levels in the $4,480-4,500 zone. This behavior reflects strong dip-buying interest even after sharp multi-session advances.

Macro and geopolitical forces sustain demand

Macro conditions remain a powerful tailwind. Expectations of further easing by the Federal Reserve have strengthened as inflation moderates and labor market data points to gradual cooling. While U.S. growth has remained resilient, markets continue to price in rate cuts in 2026, keeping real yields under pressure and enhancing gold’s relative appeal.

Geopolitical risk has added another layer of support. Rising tensions involving Venezuela and U.S. actions targeting oil shipments have lifted safe haven demand across commodities. Gold has remained a primary beneficiary, attracting defensive flows without relying solely on risk-off sentiment to sustain gains.

From a longer-term perspective, the scale of the move is historically significant. Gold is up roughly 70% this year, putting it on track for its strongest annual performance since 1979. Central bank buying has remained persistent, providing a steady structural bid, while inflows into gold-backed funds have been measured rather than speculative. This combination points to accumulation driven by strategic allocation decisions rather than late-cycle excess.

Outlook remains constructive into the new year

Overall, gold’s technical and fundamental profile remains decisively bullish. While brief consolidations or shallow pullbacks are possible given stretched momentum readings, the broader structure favors continuation as long as price holds above the $4,300-4,350 support zone.

As discussed previously in recent market coverage, gold has consistently outperformed when easing expectations converge with geopolitical stress, and the current environment reflects that same alignment. With real yields compressed and global risk sensitivity elevated, gold continues to assert itself not as a tactical trade, but as a long-term macro anchor heading into the new year.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Boston Scientific: Gangbusters Growth, And A Valuation To Match
data.description:
As I've said in the past, valuation often doesn’t matter in the short run, particularly for well-run companies offering above-average growth, but eventually, it does matter. Such has been the case with Boston Scientific Corporation (BSX) in 2025, as the company has continued to execute at a high level but has struggled to find much room to re-rate higher.

Boston Scientific shares are basically flat from my last update, underperforming a relatively lackluster medical device space in 2025, though Abbott (ABT) and Medtronic (MDT) both managed double-digit appreciation over the same time period (while Stryker (SYK) performed slightly worse).

I am still impressed by the growth that Boston Scientific offers, with leading positions in multiple large and growing medical franchises. Valuation is still the sticking point, though, and it takes some stretching to drive meaningful upside from here.

More Of The Same Seems Like A Good Bet For Q4

Nothing has happened in the fourth quarter of the year that leads me to believe that Boston Scientific will come up short when it next reports earnings. Procedure counts across major markets like electrophysiology appear to be healthy, and I don’t think there has been much in the way of news to drive unexpected share swings.

I actually see a little upside for Boston Scientific given ongoing growth in electrophysiology (Farapulse) and Watchman procedures, as well as solid trends in endoscopy and neuromodulation (including healthy growth in deep brain stimulation). With that, I think 18% reported year-over-year growth (a roughly 2% beat relative to the current average sell-side estimate) is plausible.

I’m a little less confident on gross margin, mostly just because this line item has been a bit volatile with mix shifts. More positively, management has consistently improved operating leverage for multiple quarters and effectively powered through any gross margin disappointments, so I do expect some upside on operating margin (around half a point).

As is typically the case with high-multiple growth stocks, guidance for 2026 will likely be more impactful to the share price than reported fourth-quarter results. Management laid out a case at its recent investor day for multiple years of 10%+ revenue growth coupled with margin leverage (averaging around 50bp/year for adjusted operating margin), and anything short of 11%-12% revenue growth guidance will very likely be seen as disappointing.

Growth Drivers Remain Solidly In Place

Not that many companies with $20 billion or more in revenue can credibly hope to grow at a double-digit rate for multiple years running, but Boston Scientific has an unusual roster of quality growth drivers.

Ongoing growth in pulsed field ablation (or PFA) for atrial fibrillation with the Farapulse system is a well-understood catalyst for the business. Growing share within the space (and overall double-digit growth for ablation as a category) helped drive nearly 63% electrophysiology growth in Q3’25, and I expect ongoing strength for several years, even with the entry of Medtronic (MDT) and others into the market.

Management has sized the market at $13 billion, growing 15% a year, and not only does the company have a series of product iterations to sustain growth (Farapoint, Farawave, and Faraflex), success with Farapulse is also driving adoption of the company’s Opal mapping system (roughly one in three uses it currently). As far as positive catalysts go, the read-out of the AVANT GUARD study in 1H’26 could further expand labeling/usage into front-line persistent a-fib, and that would certainly help secure future growth.

Left atrial appendage closure (or LAAC) with Watchman remains a major growth opportunity as well. The Watchman saw almost 35% year-over-year growth in Q3, with a rising contribution from concomitant procedures (ablation for a-fib). Key to the long-term opportunity is expanded utilization of the device (through successful clinical studies), as the addressable patient population could jump from around 4M today to over 18M by 2030.

To this end, the recent OCEAN-AF and CLOSURE-AF studies that showed unimpressive results for LAAC versus standard medical therapy aren’t particularly significant. Neither reflects the core patient population (today’s or 2030 projected), and success in those studies would have been unexpected upside. With that, the CHAMPION-AF read-out in 1H’26 is much more important to the franchise.

Farapulse and Watchman are likely well-understood drivers for Boston Scientific at this point, but there are numerous other strong offerings behind them that can contribute positively. The Agent drug-coated balloon has been performing well within the ICT business with double-digit growth, and Boston Scientific has catalysts in the pipeline, including the Seismiq intravascular lithotripsy catheter (2027, with the FRACTURE study reading out in 2026), TIVUS renal denervation (2028), and Vitalyst for circulatory support in high-risk PCI and shock (2028).

Neuromodulation continues to perform well, with brain stimulation in particular, and the INTREPID study confirming the sustained benefits of brain stimulation for Parkinson’s should only help that franchise. The company is also working on a next-gen Intracept for pain and a next-gen spinal stimulation device (WaveWriter), and recently acquired Nalu Medical and its peripheral nerve stimulation device – basically a new (and large) market opportunity for the company.

There’s still more beyond this, including an underappreciated intravascular oncology franchise that includes selective internal radiation therapy (radiotherapy is an arguably underappreciated growth opportunity in areas like liver and prostate cancer treatment), and embolization products. The company is also pushing toward the pulmonary embolism market (a significant market for Stryker and Penumbra (PEN)) and the abdominal aortic aneurysm market, as well as some new technologies in cardiac rhythm management that could restore some growth to this slow-growing business.

The Outlook

Competition and clinical failure remain the biggest primary risks here. I don’t dismiss the risk that Medtronic presents in markets like PFA, though I do believe the market is big enough to sustain new entrants. Likewise, Boston Scientific will not be first to market in areas like intravascular lithotripsy or renal denervation, and these are important $1 billion+ market opportunities where sales execution will matter.

As far as clinical risk goes, Boston Scientific has struggled for some time to get into the transcatheter heart valve space (where Edwards (EW) has long been strong), with disappointing clinical results setting back the franchise by several years. It is possible, then, that opportunities like renal denervation, intravascular lithotripsy, Vitalyst, and pulmonary embolism could be diminished by unfavorable clinical results.

Last but not least, there is also ongoing M&A risk with Boston Scientific. I hesitate to use the word risk, given that Boston Scientific’s M&A program has largely been rather successful, but serial M&A makes some investors nervous, and the debt ratio is creeping up. I expect Boston Scientific to remain active, likely with relatively smaller deals like Nalu and SoniVie, but I would not be shocked if the company made a move for Penumbra to add/accelerate exposure to another large growth market.

Assuming Q4 goes as I expect, Boston Scientific will have done about 6% better for revenue than I expected for 2025. That has driven higher estimates down the line as well, with my three-year revenue growth estimate now at around 11% and my five-year estimate at 10%, with a long-term annualized revenue growth estimate of almost 10% (relatively little change from my prior model apart from going out another year).

On margins, I expect adjusted EBITDA margins to approach 34% over the next five years and 36% over the next decade. This should in turn drive 20%-plus free cash flow margin in 2027 and 23%-plus margins in 2033 and longer-term annualized growth of around 14% for free cash flow.

None of this makes valuation particularly easy. Discounted cash flow still only suggests a mid-single-digit long-term annualized return. With margin and growth-driven EV/revenue, I can argue for a 7.5x forward multiple that supports a $105 fair value. While I previously used an 8.5x multiple, simply moving the calendar out a year reduces the growth rate enough to impact the “fair” multiple by a full point (equivalent to about $15/share on the fair value).

Conclusion

Expectations are my biggest concern with Boston Scientific today. There have been plenty of great med-tech growth stories in the past that ultimately saw the stock stall or underperform simply because the growth couldn’t support ever-higher re-rating, and I see that risk here. I absolutely still like this business, and it’s tempting to just keep that 8.5x multiple and $120 fair value in place to justify a more positive stance. As is, though, it’s hard to say that the growth on offer here is going unnoticed or underappreciated.
